query: clinical trials evaluating tirilazad for ischemic stroke

retrieved_docs.title: Alteplase -- Administration -- Clinical Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: The NINDS trial was a randomized trial of IV alteplase versus placebo in patients with acute ischemic stroke treated within 3 hours of symptom onset. Alteplase improved functional outcomes at three months in comparison to placebo. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Artery Infarct -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- TPA (tissue Plasminogen activator or alteplase) after an acute ischemic stroke
retrieved_docs.content: NINDS (National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke- tPA/tissue plasminogen activator/alteplase) trial [1995] reported significantly improved outcomes with the use of tPA within 3 hours of acute ischemic stroke onset, despite the increased risk of symptomatic intracerebral hemorrhage. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Cerebral Ischemia(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In the case of acute ischemic stroke, the first priority is determining whether the patient is a candidate for acute reperfusion therapy. Intravenous thrombolytics have been in use for the treatment of acute ischemic stroke for more than 25 years. Tissue plasminogen activator (TPA) is currently the only thrombolytic agent approved by the Food and Drug Administration for the management of acute ischemic stroke, though studies into the efficacy of tenecteplase are underway. [13] TPA is approved for the treatment of acute ischemic stroke if it can be administered within 3 hours of symptom onset; however, studies indicate that it may be safe and effective if given as much as 4.5 hours after the development of symptoms. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Artery Infarct -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Aspirin+Clopidogrel (dual antiplatelets) Appeared to be Superior to Aspirin Alone in Preventing Strokes after TIA/Small Ischemic Strokes
retrieved_docs.content: The POINT (Platelet-Oriented Inhibition in New TIA and Minor Ischemic Stroke) trial [2018] is a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial (including 4881 subjects) that compared aspirin monotherapy versus aspirin/clopidogrel in 269 centers in North America, Europe, Australia, and New Zealand. The bottom line suggests that in patients with acute TIA or small ischemic stroke, starting dual antiplatelet therapy (clopidogrel load 600 mg then 75 mg/d, aspirin 75 to 325mg/d, and placebo group with no other thrombolytics for 30 days) after the symptom onset decreases 90-day stroke risk at the cost of a slightly increased bleeding risk as compared to aspirin monotherapy. [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Artery Infarct -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Aspirin+Clopidogrel (dual antiplatelets) Appeared to be Superior to Aspirin Alone in Preventing Strokes after TIA/Small Ischemic Strokes
retrieved_docs.content: The ASCEND (A Study of Cardiovascular Events iN Diabetes) trial [2018] showed that the use of aspirin for primary prevention therapy reduces the risk of serious vascular events in patients with well-controlled diabetes (A1c<8; 50% of patients). [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Artery Infarct -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Aspirin+Clopidogrel (dual antiplatelets) Appeared to be Superior to Aspirin Alone in Preventing Strokes after TIA/Small Ischemic Strokes
retrieved_docs.content: The CHANCE (Clopidogrel in High-risk Patients With Acute Non-disabling Cerebrovascular Events) trial [2013] is also a randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled trial  (including 5170 subjects) that compared aspirin monotherapy versus aspirin/clopidogrel in 114 centers across China. The bottom line suggests that in patients with acute TIA or small ischemic stroke, starting dual antiplatelet therapy (clopidogrel load 300 mg then 75 mg/d, aspirin 75 to 300mg/d and placebo group with no other thrombolytics for 30 days) after the symptom onset decreases 90-day stroke risk at the cost of a slightly increased bleeding risk as compared to aspirin monotherapy. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Artery Infarct -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Aspirin+Clopidogrel (dual antiplatelets) Appeared to be Superior to Aspirin Alone in Preventing Strokes after TIA/Small Ischemic Strokes
retrieved_docs.content: Summary of POINT and CHANCE trials: In the POINT trial, the duration of treatment was longer (90 versus 30 days), and the clopidogrel dose was higher (600 versus 300), but it is unclear if this was the reason for the slightly higher bleeding rates in POINT trial. Although pooled analysis shows that after comparing these two trials, the benefit of dual-antiplatelet therapy appeared limited to 21 days after an ischemic stroke. [45]

retrieved_docs.title: Tenecteplase -- Administration -- Acute Ischemic Stroke
retrieved_docs.content: The most recently published AcT trial in Canada is a large randomized open-label trial comparing 0.25 mg/kg of tenecteplase and alteplase. This trial demonstrated the non-inferiority of tenecteplase versus alteplase. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertension Clinical Trials  -- Issues of Concern -- ONTARGET (The 2008 Ongoing Telmisartan Alone and in Combination with Ramipril Global Endpoint Trial)
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with cardiovascular disease or diabetes mellitus with complications, telmisartan is as efficacious as ramipril in preventing death, MI, and stroke. The combination of telmisartan plus ramipril had no increase in benefit but was associated with more adverse effects. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Tenecteplase -- Administration -- Acute Ischemic Stroke
retrieved_docs.content: Several randomized controlled trials for acute ischemic stroke have compared tenecteplase and alteplase. The EXTEND-1A TNK trial showed better reperfusion versus alteplase in lesions with low clot burden. [13] The NOR-TEST2 trial with 0.4 mg/kg of tenecteplase failed to show non-inferiority to a standard dose of IV alteplase. [14] The ATTEST trial compared 0.25 mg/kg of tenecteplase and the standard dose of IV alteplase and found no differences in the outcomes. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Cerebral Artery Stroke -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Beyond the acute management of stroke, the use of antihypertensives, dual antiplatelet therapy, anticoagulants, and carotid endarterectomy should be used to prevent recurrent events. Antiplatelet therapy or anticoagulants are not recommended within 24h after alteplase administration. Aspirin is not a recommendation as a substitute for other interventions for acute stroke. Administration of a glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptor inhibitor is not recommended, and a recent Cochrane review showed that these agents correlated with a high risk of intracranial hemorrhage. Dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin and clopidogrel) is recommended to start within 24 hours for 21 days in patients with minor stroke for early secondary stroke prevention. The CHANCE trial showed that the primary outcome of a recurrent stroke at 90 days favored dual antiplatelet therapy over aspirin alone (HR 0.68; 95% CI, 0.57 to 0.81, p<0.0001). Ticagrelor over aspirin in acute stroke treatment is not recommended. According to SOCRATES trial with the primary outcome of time to the composite endpoint of stroke, myocardial infarction (MI) or death up to 90 days, ticagrelor was not found to be superior to aspirin (hazard ratio [HR] 0.89, 95% CI, 0.78-1.01; p=0.07). However, ticagrelor is a reasonable alternative in patients with contraindication to aspirin. The efficacy of tirofiban and eptifibatide is currently unknown. [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Lacunar Stroke -- Treatment / Management -- Lacunar Stroke Management
retrieved_docs.content: Management of a patient who presents with acute lacunar infarct out of the tPA window consistent with a noncardioembolic stroke includes dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel within 24 hours of symptom onset and continued for 21 days. Dual antiplatelet therapy in the acute phase effectively lowers recurrent ischemic stroke for 90 days from symptom onset. This is supported by the Clopidogrel in High-Risk Patients with Acute Nondisabling Cerebrovascular Events (CHANCE) and the Platelet-Oriented Inhibition in New TIA and Minor Ischemic Stroke (POINT) trials. These studies were performed on patients with all types of ischemic stroke but supported for use in lacunar strokes in subgroup analysis. Antiplatelet therapy should be delayed for 24 hours in patients who received tPA. [34] Patients with stuttering lacunar infarcts may also benefit from intravenous tPA.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertension Clinical Trials  -- Issues of Concern -- Key Points
retrieved_docs.content: The primary outcome of cardiovascular mortality, myocardial infarction, stroke, or heart failure hospitalization was similar in both ramipril and telmisartan groups. A combination of telmisartan and ramipril, however, showed an increased rate of nephropathy. The combination group achieved about 2 to 3 mmHg reduction in SBP but did not help with the primary or the secondary outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Eptifibatide -- Indications -- Non-FDA-Labeled Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Eptifibatide is used to enhance myocardial perfusion in the ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) before PCI, as supported by the Time to Integrefilin Therapy in Acute Myocardial Infarction (TITAN)-TIMI 34 trial. [3] Another possible use of eptifibatide is to enhance the incidence and speed of reperfusion when used in large doses in combination with heparin, aspirin, and tissue plasminogen activators in STEMI patients, as evidenced by the small group in the IMPACT-AMI trial. In the IMPACT-AMI trial, using eptifibatide showed complete reperfusion and an early ST-segment recovery on the electrocardiogram. [4] Eptifibatide can be used in combination with TPA in acute ischemic strokes to prevent progression to subacute intracerebral hemorrhage, as supported by the "Combined Approach to Lysis Utilizing Eptifibatide and rt-PA in Acute Ischemic Stroke (CLEAR)" trial. [5] A recent meta-analysis demonstrated eptifibatide's potential safety and efficacy as a bridging strategy for patients undergoing surgery following coronary stent implantation. Additional randomized controlled trials are warranted to strengthen the scientific evidence and validate these findings. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertension Clinical Trials  -- Issues of Concern -- Primary Outcome
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular mortality, MI, stroke, or heart failure hospitalization:16.5% vs. 16.7% vs. 16.3% (RR 1.01; 95% CI 0.94-1.09 | RR 0.99; 95% CI 0.92-1.07) Telmisartan noninferior to ramipril (P=0.004)

retrieved_docs.title: Transient Loss of Vision -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Ongoing Research
retrieved_docs.content: Assessment of novel antiplatelet therapy for TIA: This multicenter trial is investigating the efficacy of new antiplatelet drugs in preventing recurrent ischemic events in patients with TIA, including those with transient vision loss. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2912
retrieved_docs.content: Angiotensin II Receptor Blockers (ARBs) constitute a pharmacologic class of antihypertensive agents that inhibit the binding of angiotensin II to the AT₁ receptor, thereby reducing vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sympathetic activation. The specific ARB drugs irbesartan, olmesartan medoxomil, telmisartan, and valsartan are all members of this class. By sharing the same mechanism of action, these agents are used interchangeably or sequentially in the management of hypertension, heart failure, and diabetic nephropathy, offering clinicians multiple options to tailor therapy based on efficacy, tolerability, and patient‑specific factors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2951
retrieved_docs.content: Candesartan, telmisartan, and valsartan are all classified as plain angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs). Each of these agents belongs to the broader drug class of ANGIOTENSIN II RECEPTOR BLOCKERS (ARBs), which act by selectively inhibiting the AT₁ receptor for angiotensin II. By blocking this receptor, these medications reduce vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sympathetic activation, making them effective for managing hypertension, heart failure, and protecting renal function in patients with diabetes or chronic kidney disease. Although they share the same mechanism of action and therapeutic indications, candesartan, telmisartan, and valsartan are distinct individual drugs within the ARB class, offering clinicians multiple options for individualized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2897
retrieved_docs.content: Imidapril, lisinopril, perindopril, trandolapril, and zofenopril are all members of the plain ACE inhibitor class, referred to as ace inhibitors, plain. Each of these agents shares the common mechanism of inhibiting the angiotensin‑converting enzyme, making them useful for managing conditions such as hypertension, heart failure, and other cardiovascular disorders. By belonging to the same pharmacologic group, these drugs exhibit similar therapeutic effects and safety considerations, underscoring the importance of recognizing them as a unified class in clinical decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2603
retrieved_docs.content: ACE INHIBITORS, PLAIN is a pharmacologic class comprising benazepril, delapril, enalapril, fosinopril, ramipril, trandolapril, and zofenopril. These agents act by inhibiting the angiotensin‑converting enzyme, thereby reducing the formation of angiotensin II and decreasing vasoconstriction and aldosterone secretion. As a result, they are commonly employed in the management of hypertension, heart failure, post‑myocardial infarction remodeling, and diabetic nephropathy. The inclusion of each listed drug within the ACE INHIBITORS, PLAIN class underscores their shared mechanism of action and therapeutic indications, facilitating clinicians’ selection of appropriate therapy based on patient‑specific factors such as dosing frequency, tolerability, and renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2808
retrieved_docs.content: Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs), plain, constitute a pharmacologic class of antihypertensive agents that selectively inhibit the AT₁ receptor to block the actions of angiotensin II. The individual drugs eprosartan, irbesartan, telmisartan and azilsartan medoxomil are all members of this plain ARB class. As members of the same therapeutic group, these agents share a common mechanism of reducing vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sympathetic activation, which makes them useful for managing hypertension, heart failure, and protecting renal function in patients with diabetes or chronic kidney disease. Recognizing that eprosartan, irbesartan, telmisartan and azilsartan medoxomil belong to the same ARB category helps clinicians select appropriate monotherapy or consider them as alternatives when tailoring treatment to individual patient needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1666
retrieved_docs.content: ACE inhibitors (plain) represent a pharmacologic class of angiotensin‑converting enzyme inhibitors used to treat hypertension, heart failure, and renal protection. The class includes the agents benazepril, cilazapril, fosinopril, imidapril, lisinopril, moexipril, perindopril, quinapril, spirapril, and trandolapril. All of these drugs share the mechanism of competitively inhibiting ACE, thereby reducing the production of angiotensin II, decreasing vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sympathetic activation. By lowering systemic vascular resistance and blood pressure, they improve outcomes in patients with cardiovascular disease and diabetic nephropathy. Recognizing that each of these agents belongs to the same therapeutic group assists clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy, considering pharmacokinetic differences, dosing schedules, and side‑effect profiles while maintaining the core benefits of ACE inhibition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2520
retrieved_docs.content: Candesartan, irbesartan, telmisartan and valsartan are all members of the plain angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB) class. As “plain” ARBs, each of these agents acts by selectively blocking the angiotensin II type 1 receptor, thereby inhibiting the vasoconstrictive and aldosterone‑secreting effects of angiotensin II. This pharmacologic group is commonly used to treat hypertension, heart failure, and to provide renal protection in patients with diabetes, offering a therapeutic option that does not combine the ARB with other antihypertensive agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1433
retrieved_docs.content: Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs), plain, constitute a pharmacologic class of antihypertensive agents that act by selectively blocking the AT₁ receptor, thereby inhibiting the vasoconstrictive and aldosterone‑secreting effects of angiotensin II. Candesartan, telmisartan, and azilsartan medoxomil are each members of this plain ARB class. As members of the same therapeutic group, these three drugs share a common mechanism of action and are used to lower blood pressure, reduce cardiovascular risk, and provide renal protection in conditions such as hypertension, heart failure, and diabetic nephropathy. Although they belong to the same class, each agent has distinct pharmacokinetic properties—such as differences in half‑life, bioavailability, and receptor affinity—that can influence dosing schedules and individual patient tolerability. Recognizing candesartan, telmisartan, and azilsartan medoxomil as members of the plain ARB category helps clinicians select an appropriate agent based on specific clinical scenarios, comorbidities, and patient response, thereby optimizing cardiovascular and renal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 350
retrieved_docs.content: ACE inhibitors (plain) are a pharmacologic subclass of agents acting on the renin‑angiotensin system. The class includes benazepril, cilazapril, delapril, fosinopril, imidapril, lisinopril, moexipril, perindopril, quinapril, ramipril, spirapril, trandolapril and zofenopril; perindopril, quinapril, ramipril, spirapril, trandolapril and zofenopril are specifically identified as “plain” ACE inhibitors. All members share the mechanism of inhibiting angiotensin‑converting enzyme, which blocks the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, thereby reducing angiotensin II–mediated vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion and sympathetic activation, lowering systemic vascular resistance and blood pressure. By modulating this hormonal pathway, plain ACE inhibitors are employed in the management of hypertension, heart failure, post‑myocardial‑infarction ventricular remodeling, diabetic nephropathy and other cardiovascular and renal diseases. The “plain ACE inhibitors” subgroup is a subset of the broader therapeutic group “agents acting on the renin‑angiotensin system,” allowing clinicians to select individual agents based on pharmacokinetic properties, tolerability and patient‑specific factors while maintaining a consistent therapeutic strategy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 55
retrieved_docs.content: The pharmacologic network links several chemical entities, drug classes, and therapeutic outcomes. 1H‑tetrazole, amidy and azoly are synonymous identifiers that belong to the same structural or mechanistic category as the antiplatelet agent cilostazol and are also associated with the renin‑angiotensin system inhibitor aliskiren. Aliskiren, a direct renin inhibitor, is classified in the therapeutic function “Renin‑inhibitors” and appears in fixed‑dose combinations aliskiren 300 mg/hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg oral tablet, aliskiren / amlodipine, and aliskiren / hydrochlorothiazide; all are placed within the ATC group “other agents acting on the renin‑angiotensin system.” Within the renin‑angiotensin system, aliskiren’s mechanism is contraindicated when used together with angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors, whose ACE‑inhibitor class includes agents such as rescinnamine, underscoring the need for careful patient selection. Aldosterone antagonists constitute a distinct subclass

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2791
retrieved_docs.content: Ace inhibitors, plain constitute a pharmacologic class of angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors, and this class includes the individual agents cilazapril, fosinopril, moexipril, quinapril, spirapril, and trandolapril. Each of these drugs is a member of the ace inhibitors, plain group and shares the common mechanism of blocking the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, thereby reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone secretion. By belonging to the same therapeutic class, these agents are used interchangeably in the management of conditions such as hypertension, heart failure, and diabetic nephropathy, and they exhibit similar clinical considerations regarding dosing, side‑effect profiles, and contraindications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: Candesartan, olmesartan medoxomil, valsartan, and azilsartan medoxomil are all classified as plain angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs). As members of this drug class, each of these agents selectively blocks the angiotensin II type 1 receptor, thereby inhibiting the vasoconstrictive and aldosterone‑secreting effects of angiotensin II. This mechanism makes them useful antihypertensive medications and contributes to cardiovascular protection in patients with hypertension, heart failure, or chronic kidney disease. Recognizing that these four agents share the same pharmacologic class helps clinicians consider them interchangeably or select among them based on individual patient factors such as dosing convenience, tolerability, or specific clinical indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2608
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine mesylate and kabergolin are contraindicated in patients with hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم). In contrast, several agents may be used to treat this condition: lercanidipine, terazosin hydrochloride, terazosin hydrochloride anhydrous, and torasemide each have therapeutic potential for lowering blood pressure in hypertension. Moreover, hypertension is linked to a broader category of autonomic nervous system disorders (اضطرابات الجهاز العصبي المستقل), highlighting the role of dysregulated autonomic regulation in the development of elevated arterial pressure. Recognizing both the medications that should be avoided and those that can be employed, together with the underlying autonomic mechanisms, is essential for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1653
retrieved_docs.content: Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs), plain represent a class of antihypertensive medications that function by selectively inhibiting the angiotensin II type 1 receptor, thereby reducing vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sympathetic activation. Specific agents that belong to this class include olmesartan medoxomil, telmisartan, and azilsartan medoxomil. As members of the ARB family, olmesartan medoxomil, telmisartan, and azilsartan medoxomil share a common mechanism of action and are used to manage hypertension, protect renal function in diabetic patients, and treat heart failure or left‑ventricular dysfunction. Recognizing that these three drugs are individual members of the broader “angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs), plain” category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy based on pharmacokinetic properties, dosing convenience, and patient‑specific factors while maintaining the therapeutic benefits associated with ARB therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1931
retrieved_docs.content: ACE INHIBITORS, PLAIN is a pharmacologic class of angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors that comprises the individual agents cilazapril, delapril, fosinopril, imidapril, lisinopril, moexipril and trandolapril. Each of these drugs is recognized as a member of the plain ACE inhibitor group, sharing the common mechanism of blocking the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, thereby reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone‑mediated sodium retention. This class is widely used in the management of hypertension, heart failure, and post‑myocardial infarction care, and the inclusion of all seven agents under the ACE INHIBITORS, PLAIN category reflects their unified therapeutic role.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2213
retrieved_docs.content: ANGIOTENSIN II RECEPTOR BLOCKERS (ARBs), PLAIN constitute a pharmacologic class that includes the individual agents eprosartan, irbesartan, olmesartan medoxomil, and telmisartan. Each of these plain‑form ARBs shares the common mechanism of antagonizing the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, thereby reducing vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sympathetic activation. By blocking this pathway, the agents are used to manage hypertension, protect renal function in diabetic patients, and treat heart failure or left‑ventricular dysfunction. Recognizing that eprosartan, irbesartan, olmesartan medoxomil, and telmisartan are all members of the plain ARB category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy within a unified therapeutic class while considering the specific pharmacokinetic and safety profiles of each drug.
